course_info.title: Programming Fundamentals I
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Computer Science and Python
learning_objectives: Understand what computer science is and its core subfields
Install and configure a Python development environment
Write and execute a first Python program
readings: Think Python, Ch. 1 - The Way of the Program

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Programming Fundamentals I. This course introduces you to computational thinking and programming using Python. By the end, you will be able to decompose problems, design algorithms, and implement solutions in code.
key_concepts: What is computer science: algorithms, abstraction, and automation
Variables, assignment, and basic data types (int, float, str, bool)
The print() function and string formatting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live walkthrough of installing Python 3.12 and VS Code, writing a Hello World program, and exploring the interactive interpreter
setup_requirements: VS Code with Python extension configured

review.value: Python executes code line by line through an interpreter rather than compiling to machine code first, enabling rapid prototyping and interactive exploration
A variable is a name bound to a value in memory using the = operator; Python uses dynamic typing so the type is inferred at runtime

class_schedule.topic: Control Flow: Conditionals and Boolean Logic
learning_objectives: Write if, elif, and else statements to control program flow
Combine boolean expressions using and, or, and not
Trace program execution through branching paths
readings: Think Python, Ch. 5 - Conditionals and Recursion

lecture_notes.introduction: Programs must make decisions. Conditional statements let your code choose different paths based on data, enabling programs that respond to varying inputs rather than always doing the same thing.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a simple grade calculator that takes a numeric score and outputs a letter grade using chained conditionals

review.value: Python stops evaluating a boolean expression as soon as the result is determined: 'False and X' never evaluates X, 'True or X' never evaluates X
In Python, 0, empty string, empty list, and None evaluate as False in boolean context; everything else evaluates as True

learning_objectives: Use for loops to iterate over sequences and ranges
Use while loops for condition-based repetition
Avoid infinite loops and apply break/continue correctly

lecture_notes.introduction: Loops are fundamental to programming because they let a few lines of code process thousands of data items. Mastering iteration is the single most important skill in this course.
key_concepts: The for loop and range() function
The while loop and loop conditions
Loop control: break, continue, and else clauses
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write a program that computes the sum and average of a list of numbers entered by the user, using a while loop for input and a for loop for computation
setup_requirements: Understanding of conditionals from previous session

review.value: Initialize a variable before the loop, update it inside the loop on each iteration, and use it after the loop; this pattern underlies counting, summing, finding max/min, and string building
range(n) produces values 0 through n-1; range(1, n+1) produces 1 through n. Choosing the wrong bounds is the most common loop bug

learning_objectives: Define and call functions with parameters and return values
Understand scope and the difference between local and global variables

lecture_notes.introduction: Functions are the primary tool for organizing code. A well-designed function does one thing, has a clear name, and can be tested independently. This session covers how to define, call, and compose functions.
key_concepts: def keyword, parameters, arguments, and return values
Positional vs keyword arguments, default parameter values
Function composition: calling functions from other functions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Refactor a monolithic script into well-named functions: a temperature converter that supports Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin conversions

review.value: Variables defined inside a function are local to that function and cannot be accessed outside; this prevents accidental interference between different parts of a program
return sends a value back to the caller for further computation; print() displays text on screen. Functions that return values are composable; functions that only print are not

class_schedule.topic: Lists, Tuples, and Introduction to Data Structures
learning_objectives: Create, index, slice, and modify lists
Understand the difference between mutable and immutable sequences
Use list methods and list comprehensions effectively

lecture_notes.introduction: Real programs work with collections of data, not just single values. Lists are Python's workhorse data structure, and understanding how they work is essential for everything that follows.
key_concepts: List methods: append, extend, insert, remove, sort, reverse
Aliasing vs copying: when two names point to the same list
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a student grade tracker that stores grades in a list, computes statistics, and filters using list comprehensions

review.value: Lists are mutable (elements can be changed in place); tuples are immutable (once created, elements cannot be changed). Mutability affects aliasing behavior and function side effects
[expression for item in iterable if condition] creates a new list by transforming and filtering elements in a single readable line

learning_objectives: Use dictionaries for key-value data storage and lookup
Understand sets for unique collections and membership testing
Read from and write to text files using Python

lecture_notes.introduction: Dictionaries and sets complete your toolkit for organizing data in memory. File I/O lets your programs persist data and process external datasets. Together, these enable programs that work with real-world data.
key_concepts: Dictionary methods: get, keys, values, items, update
Sets: creation, membership testing, set operations (union, intersection, difference)
Opening files: open(), read(), write(), and the with statement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a word frequency counter that reads a text file, counts word occurrences using a dictionary, and writes the results to a new file
setup_requirements: Sample text file provided on LMS

review.value: Dictionaries use hash tables internally, giving O(1) average-case lookup time; keys must be immutable (strings, numbers, tuples) so their hash values remain stable
The 'with open(filename) as f:' pattern guarantees the file is closed when the block exits, even if an exception occurs, preventing resource leaks

questions.question:
What is the output of the following code?

x = 10
y = 3
print(x // y, x % y)
questions.answer:
3 1

x // y is integer division (10 // 3 = 3), and x % y is the modulo/remainder (10 % 3 = 1).

rubric.key: Understanding that // truncates toward negative infinity

questions.question: Write a for loop that prints all even numbers from 2 to 20 inclusive.

rubric.key: Correct even number logic (step=2 or modulo check)

questions.question: Write a function called count_vowels that takes a string parameter and returns the number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u) in the string. It should be case-insensitive.
Given the list nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6], write a list comprehension that produces a new list containing only the elements greater than 3, each multiplied by 2.
questions.answer:
[x * 2 for x in nums if x > 3]
# Result: [8, 10, 18, 12]

major_assignments.title: Text Adventure Game
major_assignments.description: Build an interactive text-based adventure game in Python that uses conditionals, loops, functions, and data structures. The game should have at least 5 rooms, an inventory system, and a win condition.
objectives: Apply control flow, functions, and data structures in an integrated project
Practice decomposing a complex problem into manageable functions
Implement user input handling with validation
Create an engaging interactive experience through code
deliverables: Python source file(s) with complete game implementation
README.txt with game description and instructions
Design document (1-2 pages) explaining room layout and game logic
Screenshot or transcript of a complete playthrough

criteria: Game has at least 5 interconnected rooms with descriptions
Inventory system allows picking up and using items
Win condition is achievable and clearly communicated
Input validation prevents crashes on unexpected input
Code is organized into well-named functions
Functions have docstrings explaining their purpose
Variable names are descriptive and consistent
No duplicated code; common patterns are factored into functions
Rooms are stored in a dictionary with connections
Inventory uses a list or set appropriately
Game state is managed through clear data structures
Appropriate choice of data structure for each use case
README clearly explains how to run and play the game
Design document shows room layout and logic
Code comments explain non-obvious decisions
Playthrough demonstrates all major features
Fully functional text adventure with rooms, inventory, and win condition
Clean, well-organized code demonstrating mastery of functions and data structures
Robust input handling that never crashes on unexpected input
Complete documentation including design document and playthrough

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of programming concepts and syntax

lab_assignments.description: Weekly hands-on programming exercises building core skills

midterm_examination.description: Written and coding exam covering first half of course material

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all course material

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement with the instructor.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts is encouraged, but all code must be written independently. Sharing code or copying from any source without attribution constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted code must be your own work. Use of AI code generators must be disclosed. Plagiarism or unauthorized collaboration will result in a zero on the assignment and possible course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop with Python 3.12+ and VS Code installed. Lab computers are available but personal machines are strongly recommended for practice outside class.
